clinicalStem: A 38-year-old male office worker presents with a 6-week history of low back pain radiating down the right leg to the dorsum of the foot and big toe. He describes the pain as sharp, worsened by coughing and straining. On examination, straight leg raise reproduces his leg pain at 40 degrees on the right. Power is reduced in right ankle dorsiflexion (4/5), and sensation is decreased over the first web space of the right foot. The right knee and ankle reflexes are intact. Which nerve root is most likely compressed?
explanation: L5 radiculopathy classically produces pain and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and first web space, with weakness of ankle dorsiflexion (extensor hallucis longus). Reflexes are typically preserved as L5 does not contribute to the standard knee or ankle jerk. The positive straight leg raise at 40 degrees indicates significant nerve root tension consistent with L4/5 disc herniation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: L3 radiculopathy presents with anterior thigh pain, weakness of knee extension, and a reduced knee reflex — not consistent with this distribution to the dorsum of the foot.
L4 radiculopathy produces medial leg and ankle pain, weak knee extension and ankle dorsiflexion, and a reduced knee jerk — the diminished reflex would be the knee jerk, not absent here.
L5 is correct: dorsum of foot and first web space sensory loss plus dorsiflexion weakness with preserved reflexes is the hallmark L5 pattern.
S1 radiculopathy causes pain down the posterior leg to the lateral foot, weakness of plantarflexion and eversion, and a diminished ankle jerk — not matching this case.
S2 radiculopathy affects the posterior thigh and leg with bladder/bowel involvement in severe cases — not consistent with this focal foot and toe distribution.
clinicalPearl: L5 radiculopathy: dorsum of foot and first web space sensory loss + weak dorsiflexion (EHL) + normal reflexes — the only common lumbar level with no reliable reflex to test.
keyClue: Sensory loss over the first web space and dorsum of foot with weak ankle dorsiflexion and intact reflexes.
commonExamTrap: Students confuse L4 and L5: L4 reduces the knee jerk; L5 does not reliably reduce any standard reflex.
missedConcept: The dermatomal and myotomal localisation of L5 nerve root compression, distinct from L4 and S1.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman with a known prolapsed L4/L5 disc presents to the emergency department reporting sudden onset of bilateral leg weakness and numbness over the past 12 hours. She describes saddle anaesthesia and admits to difficulty initiating urination with overflow incontinence. On examination she has bilateral lower limb weakness (3/5 hip flexion bilaterally), absent ankle jerks, and reduced perianal sensation. Rectal tone is reduced. Which single investigation is most urgently required to confirm the diagnosis and guide management?
options: Plain radiograph of the lumbosacral spine
CT myelography of the lumbar spine
MRI of the lumbar spine without delay
Nerve conduction studies of the lower limbs
explanation: Cauda equina syndrome (CES) is a neurosurgical emergency. MRI of the lumbar spine is the investigation of choice as it provides detailed soft-tissue resolution of the disc herniation, cord/cauda equina compression, and extent of neural involvement. It must be performed urgently as delay beyond 24-48 hours from symptom onset worsens prognosis for urinary and motor recovery. CT myelography is an alternative if MRI is contraindicated but is second-line.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Plain radiographs show bony anatomy but cannot visualise disc herniation or cauda equina compression — they have no diagnostic role in confirming CES.
CT myelography is an acceptable alternative when MRI is contraindicated (e.g. pacemaker) but is invasive, requires intrathecal contrast, and is second-line to MRI.
MRI lumbar spine is correct: it is the gold-standard, non-invasive investigation that directly visualises cauda equina compression and must not be delayed in this emergency.
Nerve conduction studies are useful in chronic neuropathy workup but are far too slow to influence urgent surgical decision-making in an acute CES presentation.
Urodynamic studies assess bladder function but cannot diagnose the structural cause of CES or guide surgical decompression — they are not the priority investigation here.
clinicalPearl: Cauda equina syndrome = saddle anaesthesia + bladder dysfunction + bilateral leg weakness → MRI lumbar spine STAT; this is a neurosurgical emergency where hours matter.
keyClue: Saddle anaesthesia combined with urinary retention and bilateral lower limb weakness in a patient with known lumbar disc disease.
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT instead of MRI or delaying imaging for urodynamic studies — CES requires MRI urgently for definitive structural diagnosis before surgical decompression.
missedConcept: Cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency; MRI is the definitive investigation and must not be deferred.
redFlagReason: Cauda equina syndrome with acute bladder dysfunction and bilateral lower limb weakness is a neurosurgical emergency — delay in MRI and decompression risks permanent incontinence and paralysis.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old retired builder presents with a 9-month history of bilateral leg pain, heaviness, and numbness that comes on after walking approximately 200 metres. The symptoms are relieved by sitting down or leaning forward over a shopping trolley. He also notes relief when cycling. Peripheral pulses are normal and ankle-brachial pressure index is 0.95 bilaterally. Lumbar extension reproduces his leg symptoms. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Peripheral arterial disease with vascular claudication
Lumbar spinal stenosis causing neurogenic claudication
L4/L5 disc herniation with bilateral radiculopathy
explanation: Neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis classically presents in older patients with bilateral leg pain/heaviness on walking relieved by flexion (sitting, leaning forward, cycling) because spinal flexion increases the spinal canal diameter. Normal ABPI excludes significant peripheral arterial disease. Lumbar extension worsens stenosis by further narrowing the canal. The shopping trolley sign (leaning forward relieves symptoms) is pathognomonic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Peripheral arterial disease would show reduced ABPI (<0.9), absent or reduced pulses, and symptoms relieved by rest in any posture — not specifically by lumbar flexion. ABPI 0.95 is normal here.
Lumbar spinal stenosis with neurogenic claudication is correct: bilateral leg symptoms relieved by flexion, normal vascular studies, and the shopping trolley sign are classic.
A focal L4/L5 disc herniation typically causes unilateral radiculopathy with dermatomal sensory and motor changes — bilateral diffuse leg heaviness over months is more consistent with stenosis.
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy produces distal symmetrical sensory loss (burning/tingling) at rest without clear exertional onset and relief with positional change.
Bilateral DVT would present with swelling, redness, tenderness, and Homan's sign — it is not associated with exertional leg heaviness relieved by spinal flexion.
clinicalPearl: The shopping trolley sign — relief on leaning forward — is the hallmark of neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis; vascular claudication is relieved by rest in any position.
keyClue: Bilateral leg symptoms relieved by leaning forward (lumbar flexion) with normal peripheral vascular studies and reproduction by lumbar extension.
commonExamTrap: Confusing neurogenic and vascular claudication — normal ABPI and relief specifically with flexion (not just rest) distinguishes neurogenic from vascular.
missedConcept: Positional relief through spinal flexion increases canal diameter and is pathognomonic of neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis.

sourceLecture: Disorders Of The Spine & Spinal Cord
sourceFile: 5 Disorders Of The Spine & Spinal Cord.pdf
clinicalStem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a stab wound to the right side of his neck at C6. On examination, below the level of the injury on the right side he has loss of motor function, loss of vibration sense and proprioception, and increased reflexes with an extensor plantar response. On the left side below the injury, he has intact motor function but reduced pain and temperature sensation. Which spinal cord syndrome best describes these findings?
explanation: Brown-Séquard syndrome results from hemisection of the spinal cord. On the ipsilateral side (right), there is motor loss (corticospinal tract) and loss of dorsal column sensation (vibration/proprioception) with UMN signs. On the contralateral side (left), there is loss of pain and temperature (spinothalamic tract crosses within 1-2 segments of entry). This crossed sensory/motor dissociation is pathognomonic of Brown-Séquard syndrome.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Anterior cord syndrome causes bilateral motor loss and bilateral loss of pain/temperature below the level, with preserved dorsal column function — not the unilateral crossed pattern seen here.

Central cord syndrome typically affects the upper limbs more than lower limbs with variable sensory loss and bladder dysfunction — it does not produce the clear ipsilateral/contralateral dissociation seen here.
Posterior cord syndrome produces bilateral loss of vibration, proprioception and fine touch below the lesion with preserved motor and pain/temperature function — not matching this case.
Brown-Séquard syndrome is correct: ipsilateral UMN signs and dorsal column loss plus contralateral spinothalamic loss is the defining crossed pattern of spinal cord hemisection.
Complete spinal cord injury would cause bilateral loss of all modalities and bilateral flaccid paralysis acutely — there would be no preserved function on either side below the level.
clinicalPearl: Brown-Séquard syndrome: ipsilateral motor loss + ipsilateral dorsal column loss (vibration/proprioception) + contralateral spinothalamic loss (pain/temperature) — the crossed sensory pattern is the diagnostic hallmark.
keyClue: Ipsilateral motor and dorsal column loss with contralateral spinothalamic sensory loss below the level of a lateral spinal injury.
commonExamTrap: Students confuse which tract crosses at which level: spinothalamic fibres cross within 1-2 segments (contralateral loss), while corticospinal and dorsal columns remain ipsilateral until medulla.
missedConcept: The crossed sensory-motor dissociation in Brown-Séquard syndrome reflects the differential crossing levels of spinal tracts.

sourceLecture: Disorders Of The Spine & Spinal Cord
sourceFile: 5 Disorders Of The Spine & Spinal Cord.pdf
topic: Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord
clinicalStem: A 61-year-old woman with a history of autoimmune hypothyroidism presents with a 4-month history of progressive symmetrical tingling and numbness in both feet and hands, worsening balance, and difficulty walking in the dark. On examination she has absent ankle jerks, upgoing plantar responses bilaterally, and a positive Romberg's sign. She appears pale with a smooth tongue. Her MBC shows macrocytic anaemia (MCV 108 fL). Which single investigation is most likely to confirm the underlying diagnosis?
explanation: This presentation is classic for subacute combined degeneration of the cord (SACD) due to vitamin B12 deficiency: dorsal column signs (sensory ataxia, Romberg positive), corticospinal tract signs (upgoing plantars), and peripheral neuropathy (absent ankle jerks), combined with macrocytic anaemia and glossitis. The autoimmune thyroid history raises suspicion for pernicious anaemia. Serum B12 is the first confirmatory investigation; if borderline, methylmalonic acid and homocysteine should be added.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Nerve conduction studies would confirm peripheral neuropathy but not the central dorsal column involvement, and would not identify the underlying nutritional cause requiring specific treatment.
MRI brain is not the priority here; MRI spine (showing posterior and lateral column high signal) would be more relevant to SACD, but the treatable cause should be confirmed first with serum B12.

Serum vitamin B12 is correct: macrocytic anaemia + dorsal column + corticospinal signs + peripheral neuropathy in an autoimmune patient is SACD until proven otherwise, and B12 is the direct confirmatory test.
Anti-dsDNA is specific for systemic lupus erythematosus, which does not explain this pattern of macrocytic anaemia with mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs and sensory ataxia.

CSF examination would be considered if inflammatory myelitis (e.g. MS) were suspected, but the macrocytic anaemia and clinical context strongly point to a metabolic/nutritional cause testable with serum B12.
clinicalPearl: SACD from B12 deficiency causes a paradox of combined UMN (upgoing plantars) and LMN (absent ankle jerks) signs plus dorsal column loss — macrocytic anaemia and glossitis are the diagnostic clue.
keyClue: Macrocytic anaemia + smooth tongue + combined UMN/LMN signs + sensory ataxia in a patient with autoimmune disease.
commonExamTrap: Students may focus on nerve conduction studies for the peripheral neuropathy and miss the central dorsal column involvement pointing to B12 deficiency.
missedConcept: B12 deficiency causes SACD affecting dorsal (proprioception/vibration) and lateral (corticospinal) columns simultaneously, explaining the mixed UMN/LMN picture.

sourceLecture: Disorders Of The Spine & Spinal Cord
sourceFile: 5 Disorders Of The Spine & Spinal Cord.pdf
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of painless burns and cuts on her hands that she often does not notice. She also reports mild weakness in both hands. On examination she has bilateral loss of pain and temperature sensation over the shoulders, upper arms, and chest in a shawl-like distribution, with preserved light touch and proprioception in the same areas. She has wasting of the small hand muscles. Lower limb examination is normal. Which diagnosis best explains this dissociated sensory loss pattern?
options: Multiple sclerosis affecting the cervical cord
explanation: Syringomyelia is characterised by a fluid-filled cavity in the central spinal cord that disrupts the decussating spinothalamic fibres (crossing through the anterior commissure), producing a cape/shawl distribution of dissociated sensory loss — pain and temperature lost, light touch and proprioception (carried in dorsal columns peripheral to the syrinx) preserved. Painless injuries (burns, cuts) result from the loss of protective pain sensation. Hand muscle wasting from anterior horn cell involvement is also characteristic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MS typically produces patchy, asymmetric sensory and motor deficits that evolve in discrete attacks, with internuclear ophthalmoplegia and optic neuritis — not a symmetric cape distribution with preserved dorsal column function.
Cervical disc herniation causes radicular pain, dermatome-specific sensory loss, and often asymmetric motor deficits at specific levels — not the bilateral cape-pattern dissociated loss seen here.

Syringomyelia is correct: central cord cavitation selectively destroys the crossing spinothalamic fibres at multiple levels producing the bilateral shawl/cape sensory dissociation with preserved dorsal columns.
Peripheral neuropathy typically causes distal glove-and-stocking sensory loss affecting all modalities, not a proximal cape distribution with modality dissociation.
ALS (motor neurone disease) causes progressive motor weakness with UMN and LMN signs but does not produce sensory loss — sensory symptoms are a negative criterion for ALS.
clinicalPearl: Syringomyelia = cape/shawl distribution of dissociated sensory loss (pain and temperature lost, dorsal column sensation preserved) + painless hand injuries + small hand muscle wasting.
keyClue: Bilateral cape-distribution dissociation of pain/temperature loss with preserved light touch/proprioception in the same areas.
commonExamTrap: Confusing syringomyelia with MS — MS produces patchy asymmetric lesions, not the symmetric cape pattern with pure spinothalamic involvement.
missedConcept: The syrinx damages crossing spinothalamic fibres in the anterior commissure first, explaining selective loss of pain/temperature with sparing of dorsal column modalities.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old retired carpenter presents with a 1-year history of progressive hand clumsiness and difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning shirts, typing), worsening balance, and leg stiffness. He denies neck pain. On examination he has wasting of the intrinsic hand muscles, hyperreflexia in the lower limbs, bilateral upgoing plantars, and a broad-based gait. Lhermitte's sign is positive. Cervical spine X-ray shows multilevel disc space narrowing and osteophytes. Which investigation will most accurately define the extent of cord compression and guide surgical planning?
options: Repeat cervical X-ray in flexion and extension
CT scan of the cervical spine
Bone scan of the cervical spine
explanation: Cervical spondylotic myelopathy (CSM) requires MRI of the cervical spine for definitive diagnosis and surgical planning. MRI directly visualises the spinal cord, degree of compression, signal change within the cord (indicating myelopathic injury), and the extent of osteophytic/disc impingement. CT shows bony anatomy but cannot adequately assess cord signal or soft-tissue compression. EMG/NCS may identify associated radiculopathy but cannot characterise cord compression.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Flexion-extension X-rays assess instability but provide no information about cord signal, soft-tissue stenosis degree, or the myelopathic changes within the cord substance.

CT cervical spine demonstrates bony osteophytes and canal dimensions well but is inferior to MRI for visualising the spinal cord itself and intrinsic cord signal changes that guide urgency of surgery.

MRI cervical spine is correct: it is the gold standard for CSM, visualising cord compression, intrinsic cord T2 signal change (indicating established myelopathy), and extent of disease for surgical planning.
EMG/NCS can detect radiculopathy and differentiate from peripheral neuropathy but cannot diagnose cord compression or quantify myelopathy — not useful for surgical planning of CSM.
Bone scan identifies metabolically active bony lesions (e.g. metastases, infection) but does not visualise cord compression or soft-tissue anatomy relevant to CSM.
clinicalPearl: MRI is the gold standard for cervical spondylotic myelopathy: it identifies cord compression AND intrinsic T2 signal change (which predicts poor surgical outcome if present long-term).
keyClue: Progressive hand clumsiness + UMN lower limb signs + positive Lhermitte's + osteophytes on X-ray in an older patient.
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT instead of MRI — CT is excellent for bony detail but cannot show cord signal change, which is critical for diagnosis and prognosis in myelopathy.
missedConcept: MRI is essential in cervical myelopathy not only to confirm compression but to identify intramedullary T2 signal changes that indicate established cord injury.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman with known breast cancer presents with a 3-week history of progressive mid-thoracic back pain, worse at night and on lying down, followed by bilateral leg weakness and difficulty walking. She now reports urinary hesitancy. On examination she has bilateral lower limb hyperreflexia, bilateral upgoing plantars, and a sensory level at T6. There is point tenderness over the T5 vertebra. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Epidural spinal cord compression from vertebral metastasis
explanation: Epidural spinal cord compression (ESCC) from vertebral metastasis is the most likely diagnosis in a patient with known breast cancer presenting with progressive nocturnal back pain, a thoracic sensory level, bilateral UMN signs, and vertebral point tenderness. Breast, lung, and prostate are the most common primaries causing vertebral metastases. Point tenderness over the vertebra reflects bony involvement, and night pain worsening on recumbency is characteristic of bony metastatic disease. This is a neurological emergency.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Thoracic disc herniation is rare, typically presents with radicular chest wall pain rather than a complete sensory level, and does not explain the oncological history or nocturnal vertebral tenderness.
Transverse myelitis is an inflammatory condition presenting acutely with rapid bilateral motor and sensory loss — it does not explain bony point tenderness or the known malignancy history.
Epidural spinal cord compression from vertebral metastasis is correct: known cancer + nocturnal back pain + vertebral tenderness + UMN signs + sensory level = ESCC until proven otherwise.
Spinal cord astrocytoma is an intramedullary primary tumour, typically presenting more insidiously in younger patients without bony involvement or an underlying systemic malignancy.
Thoracic meningioma is a benign intradural extramedullary tumour that grows slowly and would not typically present with rapid progression or vertebral point tenderness from bony erosion.
clinicalPearl: Back pain in a cancer patient is metastatic spinal cord compression until proven otherwise — nocturnal pain, vertebral tenderness, and bilateral UMN signs demand urgent MRI and oncological management.
keyClue: Known breast cancer + nocturnal progressive back pain + vertebral point tenderness at T5 + bilateral UMN signs with sensory level.
commonExamTrap: Attributing back pain in a cancer patient to benign musculoskeletal causes — ESCC must always be the working diagnosis until excluded by MRI.
missedConcept: Epidural spinal cord compression from vertebral metastasis is a oncological emergency requiring urgent MRI and high-dose corticosteroids with radiotherapy/surgical decompression.
redFlagReason: Metastatic epidural spinal cord compression is a neurological emergency — untreated progression leads to permanent paralysis and incontinence; urgent MRI and high-dose dexamethasone are required immediately.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of neck pain radiating to the right thumb and index finger, with associated tingling in the same distribution. She describes worsening with neck extension and right lateral flexion. On examination, the Spurling test is positive on the right. Power is reduced in right elbow flexion and wrist extension (4/5). The right biceps reflex is diminished; the right brachioradialis reflex is also reduced. The left side is normal. Which cervical nerve root is most likely affected?
explanation: C6 radiculopathy presents with pain and paraesthesia to the thumb and index finger (C6 dermatome), weakness of elbow flexion and wrist extension (C6 myotome), and reduced biceps and brachioradialis reflexes (both C6-mediated). The Spurling test (neck extension and lateral flexion towards the affected side reproduces arm symptoms) is highly specific for cervical radiculopathy. This distribution is classically C5/6 disc herniation compressing the C6 root.
wrongAnswerExplanations: C4 radiculopathy causes neck and shoulder pain without arm or hand dermatomal symptoms — it does not produce thumb/index finger sensory changes or biceps reflex loss.
C5 radiculopathy produces lateral arm pain, deltoid weakness, and a reduced biceps reflex, but the sensory distribution does not reach the thumb — it covers the lateral upper arm.
C6 is correct: thumb and index finger sensory changes + biceps and brachioradialis reflex loss + elbow flexion weakness is the classic C6 pattern.
C7 radiculopathy causes pain to the middle finger, weakness of elbow extension (triceps), and a reduced triceps reflex — not matching this case.
C8 radiculopathy produces medial forearm and little/ring finger symptoms with weakness of intrinsic hand muscles and finger flexion — not consistent with thumb and index finger involvement.
clinicalPearl: C6 radiculopathy: thumb + index finger paraesthesia + reduced biceps and brachioradialis reflexes + Spurling positive — the two reflexes lost (biceps and brachioradialis) both identify C6.
keyClue: Thumb and index finger sensory symptoms + reduced biceps and brachioradialis reflexes + positive Spurling test.
commonExamTrap: C5 vs C6 confusion — C5 causes lateral arm symptoms and reduced biceps reflex alone; C6 extends sensation to the thumb/index and also reduces the brachioradialis reflex.
missedConcept: Both the biceps and brachioradialis reflexes are C6-mediated, making their combined reduction a specific marker for C6 root involvement.

sourceLecture: Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System
sourceFile: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System.pdf
clinicalStem: A 28-year-old man presents with progressive bilateral leg weakness over 5 days, ascending to involve his arms. Three weeks ago he had a self-limiting diarrhoeal illness. On examination he has symmetrical flaccid weakness in all limbs (power 3/5 distally), absent deep tendon reflexes throughout, and mild glove-and-stocking sensory loss. Vital capacity on spirometry is 2.4 L (normal for his size: 4.5 L). Which CSF finding would be most consistent with the suspected diagnosis?
options: High protein, high white cell count, normal glucose
Normal protein, high white cell count, low glucose
High protein, normal white cell count (albuminocytological dissociation)
Normal protein, normal white cell count, low glucose
High protein, high white cell count, low glucose
explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) classically produces albuminocytological dissociation in the CSF — markedly elevated protein (due to nerve root inflammation and increased permeability) with a normal or near-normal white cell count. This contrasts with infectious meningitis where both protein and white cells are elevated. The clinical picture of ascending flaccid paralysis with areflexia following a gastrointestinal infection (Campylobacter jejuni is the most common preceding pathogen) is classic for GBS. The reduced vital capacity flags respiratory compromise risk.
wrongAnswerExplanations: High protein with high white cell count and normal glucose would suggest viral meningitis or early bacterial meningitis — not the pattern in GBS, which characteristically lacks pleocytosis.
High white cells with low glucose is the pattern of bacterial meningitis — this would represent an active CNS infection rather than the post-infectious autoimmune demyelination of GBS.
Albuminocytological dissociation (high protein, normal white cells) is the hallmark CSF finding in GBS — this is the correct answer.
Normal protein and normal white cells with low glucose does not represent any classic pattern and would not be consistent with the clinical diagnosis of GBS.
High protein, high white cells, and low glucose is the classic pattern of bacterial meningitis — the combination of low glucose and pleocytosis specifically argues against GBS.
clinicalPearl: GBS CSF = albuminocytological dissociation: protein elevated (often >1 g/L), white cell count normal — this paradox reflects nerve root inflammation without meningeal infection.
keyClue: Post-infectious ascending flaccid paralysis with areflexia — CSF albuminocytological dissociation confirms GBS.
commonExamTrap: Expecting a high white cell count with high protein — bacterial meningitis has both, but GBS specifically has high protein with normal/near-normal cells (the dissociation).
missedConcept: Albuminocytological dissociation in GBS occurs because nerve root inflammation raises protein without triggering meningeal cellular response.

sourceLecture: Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System
sourceFile: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System.pdf
clinicalStem: A 42-year-old obese woman presents with a 4-month history of nocturnal tingling and numbness in the right thumb, index finger, middle finger, and the radial half of the ring finger. She wakes at night and relieves her symptoms by shaking her hand. She works as a supermarket cashier performing repetitive wrist movements. Tinel's sign is positive over the right carpal tunnel. Phalen's test reproduces her symptoms in 30 seconds. Which investigation would best confirm the diagnosis?
options: Plain X-ray of the right wrist
Nerve conduction studies of the median nerve
Cervical spine MRI to exclude C6 radiculopathy
explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) is the most common entrapment neuropathy and is confirmed by nerve conduction studies (NCS) demonstrating prolonged median nerve distal motor and sensory latencies across the wrist. NCS quantifies the severity of compression, distinguishes CTS from other causes of hand paraesthesia, and guides surgical vs. conservative management. Tinel's and Phalen's signs support the clinical diagnosis but are not sufficient alone.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Plain X-ray of the wrist identifies bony causes of canal narrowing (e.g. fracture, arthritic changes) but cannot directly assess nerve function or confirm the electrophysiological diagnosis of CTS.

MRI wrist can visualise median nerve compression and other soft-tissue pathology but is expensive, less accessible, and rarely required when NCS is available — not the confirmatory investigation of choice.
Nerve conduction studies are correct: they directly measure median nerve conduction velocity and distal latencies, providing objective electrophysiological confirmation and severity grading.

Ultrasound can show median nerve enlargement at the wrist and is useful when NCS is unavailable, but NCS remains the gold standard and is more sensitive for electrophysiological severity classification.

Cervical spine MRI would be appropriate if C6 radiculopathy were suspected, but the combination of nocturnal hand flapping relief, positive Tinel/Phalen, and median nerve distribution strongly localises to the carpal tunnel.
clinicalPearl: Carpal tunnel syndrome is confirmed by nerve conduction studies showing prolonged median nerve distal latency at the wrist — this also quantifies severity and guides surgical decision-making.
keyClue: Nocturnal hand flapping relief + median nerve distribution (thumb, index, middle, radial ring finger) + positive Tinel and Phalen.
commonExamTrap: Ordering cervical spine imaging instead of NCS — the nocturnal flapping relief and carpal tunnel provocation signs clearly localise to the wrist, not the neck.
missedConcept: NCS is the gold standard for diagnosing CTS and grading severity; clinical signs support but do not replace electrophysiological confirmation.

sourceLecture: Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System
sourceFile: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System.pdf
clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man with a 12-year history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus presents with a 2-year history of burning pain, tingling, and numbness in both feet and lower legs. More recently he has noticed similar symptoms developing in his fingertips. He also describes postural dizziness. On examination there is distal symmetrical loss of pinprick, vibration, and temperature sensation in a stocking-and-glove distribution with absent ankle jerks bilaterally. His HbA1c is 10.2%. Blood pressure drops by 28 mmHg on standing. Which pattern of diabetic neuropathy does this presentation most represent?
options: Distal symmetrical sensorimotor polyneuropathy with autonomic neuropathy
Acute painful neuropathy of rapid glycaemic correction
explanation: This presentation is the classic pattern of diabetic distal symmetrical sensorimotor polyneuropathy (the most common diabetic neuropathy) combined with autonomic neuropathy (postural hypotension with >20 mmHg drop). Stocking-and-glove sensory loss with absent ankle jerks reflects length-dependent axonal degeneration. The gradual proximal extension to the fingertips follows the characteristic length-dependent progression. Postural hypotension confirms autonomic involvement, a significant marker of cardiovascular risk.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Mononeuropathy multiplex involves acute, asymmetric involvement of multiple named nerve territories (e.g. median, common peroneal) — not the symmetric distal sensory-motor pattern seen here.

Diabetic amyotrophy presents with acute severe asymmetric proximal leg pain, weakness, and wasting (typically in elderly men) with minimal sensory involvement — distinct from this symmetric distal neuropathy.

Distal symmetrical sensorimotor polyneuropathy with autonomic neuropathy is correct: length-dependent stocking-glove pattern with absent ankle jerks plus orthostatic hypotension confirms this combined presentation.
Diabetic cranial neuropathy affects isolated cranial nerves (most commonly CN III, VI), presenting with acute diplopia — not a peripheral stocking-glove sensory syndrome.

Acute painful neuropathy of rapid glycaemic correction presents acutely after rapid glucose reduction, with burning pain but normal reflexes and examination initially — not a 2-year established polyneuropathy.
clinicalPearl: The most common diabetic neuropathy is length-dependent distal symmetrical polyneuropathy — longest nerves affected first, ascending proximally; autonomic neuropathy (postural hypotension) is a serious comorbidity indicating cardiovascular risk.
keyClue: Symmetric stocking-glove sensory loss with absent ankle jerks + postural hypotension in a patient with long-standing poorly controlled diabetes.
commonExamTrap: Missing the autonomic component (postural hypotension) and classifying only the peripheral pattern — combined sensorimotor and autonomic neuropathy is a distinct and prognostically important diabetic complication.
missedConcept: Diabetic neuropathy commonly involves multiple systems simultaneously: peripheral sensorimotor and autonomic — screening for autonomic features is essential.
